source: Future Policy in Malaya - Organisation of Labour Department__CO273:675:20
filename: Future Policy in Malaya - Organisation of Labour Department__CO273:675:20.txt
summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** Colonial Office planning and correspondence on **post-war labour policy in Malaya**, focusing on the **organisation of the Labour Department(s)** and the staffing needed to manage industrial relations, trade unions, and labour unrest.  
- **Geography/time period:** Concerns **Malaya (Malayan Union and Singapore)** in the **1944–1946** period (planning during/after WWII; post-liberation administration).  
- **Major organisational decisions/proposals:** Repeated debate over structure led to plans for a **unified labour set-up under a “common head/Commissioner for Labour”**, while also recognising **two territorial administrations (Malayan Union and Singapore)** with separate departmental responsibilities but coordinated arrangements. A Feb 1946 message proposed **two separate Commissioners** (Malayan Union and Singapore) rather than a single combined head.  
- **Key staffing actions (industrial relations/trade unions):** Agreement to seek an **Industrial Relations Adviser** (initially intended via **secondment from the UK Ministry of Labour**, suggested age limit ~40, tour ~3 years, salary often cited as **$1,000/month**), plus **trade union advisers/officers** to support union organisation and conciliation work (including posts for Singapore and Malaya).  
- **Brazier appointment and pay details:** Due to difficulty recruiting via the Ministry of Labour (including issues about whether an officer’s **wife could accompany him**), the Governor recommended appointing **J.A. Brazier** (already in Malaya) instead. The Secretary of State approved offering him **Industrial Relations Adviser** on **$700–25–800/month** (telegram **No. 186, 18 Apr 1946**). Later (Oct 1946) Malayan Union sought approval to create a **joint Trades Union Adviser** post for Malayan Union & Singapore and appoint **Brazier** on **$900–25–1,000/month**, pensionable, effective **1 Oct 1946**.  
- **Labour unrest context prompting urgency:** B.M.A.(M) reported deteriorating labour conditions, strike activity and intimidation; a **railway strike lasting 34 days** is mentioned (telegram received **7 Mar 1946**). Policy emphasis included developing **collective bargaining** and using existing **Industrial Courts Laws 1940** for conciliation/arbitration; no additional conciliation machinery was considered necessary as of **Oct 1946**.  
- **Key actors/organisations:** **Colonial Office** (e.g., Sir Edward/G.E.J. Gent, Sir George Gater, Paskin, Edmonds, Morgan, Jeffries), **B.M.A.(M)** and **SEAC** (Major-General **H.R. Hone**, Major **Orde-Browne**, Duckworth, Colonel Hay), **Ministry of Labour**, plus references to the **India Office**, **Foreign Office**, and external stakeholders such as the **Rubber Growers’ Association**; early planning also involved possible labour sourcing from **India** and the **Netherlands East Indies (Java)**.
